response:
### Study Findings and Biological Mechanisms

#### Introduction
The study on DeepGrade in Ethiopia evaluated the performance of an AI-assisted breast cancer grading model using H&E-stained slides from breast cancer patients. The primary objective was to assess the accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity of the DeepGrade model in distinguishing between grade 1 and grade 3 tumours, as well as to classify Nottingham Histologic Grade 2 tumours into two distinct risk categories. The results demonstrated that the DeepGrade model showed promising performance, particularly in reducing variability in breast cancer grading.

#### Performance Metrics
The DeepGrade model achieved a 100% specificity for low-grade tumours (grades 1 and 2) and an 82.05% sensitivity for high-grade tumours (grade 3). These results were validated using metrics such as the Area Under the Curve (AUC), which was 0.914, indicating a strong predictive power. Additionally, the model showed a high agreement level of 86.79% with the pathologists' diagnoses, with a Kappa coefficient of 0.71 (95% CI: 0.51-0.90). This high agreement suggests that the AI model is reliable and can be used to complement human expertise.

#### Biomarkers and Pathways Involved
The study utilized H&E-stained slides, which contain various biomarkers and cellular components that are indicative of different histologic grades.
